viewSummary: Laura Friedman raised $1.8M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 90% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($1.3M itemized, $309K PAC), while 9% ($171K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $35K.

viewSummary: Mark Warner raised $4.3M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. 1 legal action(s)/lawsuit(s) on record.

viewSummary: Tina Smith raised $1.5M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 73% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($769K itemized, $304K PAC), while 25% ($371K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $35K.

name: Elissa Slotkin
title: Senator
viewSummary: Elissa Slotkin raised $51.9M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 67% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($29.7M itemized, $1.2M PAC), while 32% ($16.8M) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $45K.

name: Tim Sheehy
title: Senator
viewSummary: Tim Sheehy raised $30.0M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 62% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($13.9M itemized, $1.3M PAC), while 26% ($7.9M) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal.

viewSummary: Tim Scott raised $18.9M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). viewSummary: Brian Schatz raised $525K in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 79% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($285K itemized, $127K PAC), while 7% ($34K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $18K.

viewSummary: Jacky Rosen raised $43.9M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $290K.

viewSummary: Gary Peters raised $3.2M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 78% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($1.5M itemized, $881K PAC), while 22% ($712K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $96K.

viewSummary: Jon Ossoff raised $7.2M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 43% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($2.8M itemized, $4K PAC), while 56% ($4.0M) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix is grassroots-leaning, a lower influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $35K.

viewSummary: Lisa Murkowski raised $689K in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 89% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($328K itemized, $277K PAC), while 4% ($29K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $12K.

viewSummary: Mike Lee raised $5.1M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 39% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($1.9M itemized, $85K PAC), while 60% ($3.1M) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix is grassroots-leaning, a lower influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $20K.

viewSummary: Tim Kaine raised $18.3M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 72% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($6.1M itemized, $2.1M PAC), while 26% ($4.8M) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $223K.

viewSummary: John Hoeven raised $531K in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 95% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($192K itemized, $214K PAC), while 5% ($26K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $37K. 10 legal action(s)/lawsuit(s) on record.

viewSummary: Mazie Hirono raised $3.3M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 84% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($1.6M itemized, $1.1M PAC), while 15% ($504K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $166K. 2 legal action(s)/lawsuit(s) on record.

viewSummary: John Hickenlooper raised $1.2M in reported campaign funds (through 2024). About 79% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($720K itemized, $114K PAC), while 18% ($209K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal. Tracked industry/PAC contributions identified so far total $10K. 3 legal action(s)/lawsuit(s) on record.

name: Nancy Pelosi

lawsuits.title: McCarthy v. Pelosi (House proxy-voting challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed; court held the Speech or Debate Clause barred the suit. Sued as Speaker, not personal liability.
lawsuits.description: GOP Leader Kevin McCarthy and members challenged the House rule allowing proxy voting during COVID-19. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity as Speaker.

lawsuits.title: Massie v. Pelosi (proxy voting / floor fines challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed; affirmed by the D.C. Circuit on June 30, 2023 (No. 22-5058). Pelosi sued in her official capacity only.
lawsuits.description: Rep. Thomas Massie and others challenged House proxy voting and floor mask/metal-detector fines. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity as Speaker.

lawsuits.title: Budowich v. Pelosi (Jan. 6 Committee subpoena challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed. Challenge to a House Jan. 6 Select Committee subpoena; Pelosi named as Speaker.
lawsuits.description: Taylor Budowich sued to block a Select Committee subpoena for bank records. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity.

lawsuits.title: Meadows v. Pelosi (Jan. 6 Committee subpoena challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed. Challenge to a House Jan. 6 Select Committee subpoena; Pelosi named as Speaker.
lawsuits.description: Former WH Chief of Staff Mark Meadows sued to block Select Committee subpoenas. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity.

lawsuits.title: Republican National Committee v. Pelosi (Jan. 6 subpoena challenge)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed. Challenge to a Select Committee subpoena of vendor records; Pelosi named as Speaker.
lawsuits.description: The RNC sued to block a Jan. 6 Select Committee subpoena to a third-party vendor. Pelosi named as defendant in her official capacity.

About 90% came from large donors, PACs and committee transfers ($3.2M itemized, $1.4M PAC), while 10% ($525K) came from small grassroots donors. This funding mix leans heavily on big-money sources, a higher influence-risk signal.

name: Donald J. Trump
title: President of the United States

lawsuits.title: People of the State of New York v. Trump (NY "hush money" criminal case)
lawsuits.outcome: Convicted May 2024 on 34 felony counts; sentenced Jan 2025 to unconditional discharge.
lawsuits.description: First U.S. president convicted of a felony; payments related to 2016 election.

lawsuits.title: Carroll v. Trump II (defamation & sexual abuse)
lawsuits.outcome: Jury found Trump liable; $5 million in damages.
lawsuits.description: Federal jury found Trump sexually abused and defamed writer E. Jean Carroll.

lawsuits.title: Carroll v. Trump I (defamation)
lawsuits.outcome: Jury awarded $83.3 million; affirmed by Second Circuit.
lawsuits.description: Damages for statements denying Carroll's allegations.

lawsuits.title: People of the State of New York v. Trump (civil fraud)
lawsuits.outcome: ~$454M penalty later vacated by appellate court as excessive (8th Amendment); fraud liability upheld.
lawsuits.description: NY AG action over inflated asset valuations; monetary judgment thrown out on appeal.

lawsuits.title: United States v. Trump (classified documents)
lawsuits.outcome: Dismissed July 15, 2024 (Judge Cannon ruled special counsel appointment unlawful).
lawsuits.description: Charges over retention of classified documents at Mar-a-Lago.

lawsuits.title: United States v. Trump (2020 election interference) & Georgia RICO case
lawsuits.outcome: Federal case wound down after 2024 election; Georgia case largely stalled.
lawsuits.description: Charges related to efforts to overturn the 2020 election results.

sources: FEC committee totals (Donald J. Trump for President 2024, Inc.)
funding.note: Breakdown is an estimate; itemized vs. small-dollar split for the principal committee is approximate. JFC/super-PAC money (combined ~$1.45B) sits outside the candidate committee.

viewSummary: Donald J. Trump's 2024 principal campaign committee reported about $496M in receipts; counting joint fundraising committees and aligned super PACs, the broader operation raised roughly $1.45 billion (the itemized vs. small-dollar split shown is an estimate). As an executive-branch official he casts no congressional roll-call votes and files no congressional stock disclosures, so those categories are not applicable. His legal record is extensive: a 2024 New York criminal conviction on 34 felony counts, two E. Jean Carroll civil judgments ($5M and $83.3M), a New York civil fraud case (penalty later vacated on appeal, liability upheld), and federal/state cases that were dismissed or wound down.
